nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, fast-flowing, highly oxygenated headwaters and minor tributaries with rocky substrate; cobble, pebble, sand, and gravel beds covered in biofilm and algae; Pearl River system and adjacent drainages in southern China (Guangdong, Guangxi, Yunnan, Guizhou, Hunan, Jiangxi), Hong Kong, Hainan Island, and northeastern Vietnam

decorPreferences: smooth water-worn rocks and flat stones for grazing and resting
strong flow from powerhead or wavemaker (10–15× turnover per hour)
strong lighting to promote algae and diatom growth on hard surfaces

diet.notes: Aufwuchs grazer: scrapes biofilm, diatoms, algae (including Cyanobacteria), and microorganisms from hard surfaces. In aquaria, supplement with algae wafers, spirulina tabs, blanched vegetables (zucchini, cucumber), and sinking gel foods. Occasional frozen or live small invertebrates (bloodworm, Daphnia) accepted. Adequate biofilm on tank surfaces is essential; fish frequently starve in low-flow or newly set-up tanks that lack established algae growth. Biologically mature tanks with algae-coated rocks greatly improve long-term survival.

goodWith: other hillstream loaches sharing similar flow and temperature requirements
small cool-water cyprinids (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, Danios)
small peaceful loaches tolerant of high flow
small rasboras in cooler end of their range
Amano shrimp and larger ornamental shrimp in high-flow setups
avoidWith: boisterous or fin-nipping species that outcompete for food
slow-water species requiring low flow (gouramis, bettas, fancy guppies)
Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158) in small tanks — territorial competition likely between similar bottom-dwelling grazers

summary: A compact Chinese and Vietnamese hillstream loach that clings to rocks in fast-moving rapids, grazing constantly on biofilm and aufwuchs. Demands cool water (20–24°C), very high flow, and high dissolved oxygen; frequently starves in low-flow community setups. Distinguished from the Butterfly Hillstream Loach by its broken horizontal bar pattern.
careNotes:
The Broken-Band Hillstream Loach is a rheophilic specialist native to fast-flowing, highly oxygenated mountain streams across southern China (Pearl River system and adjacent drainages), Vietnam, and Hong Kong. It is named for the broken horizontal bars along its flanks, distinguishing it from the more commonly traded Butterfly Hillstream Loach (Beaufortia kweichowensis, fw-fish-158), which displays a striped and spotted dorsal pattern.

Successful long-term care hinges on replicating its natural rapids habitat. Filtration should achieve 10–15× tank volume turnover per hour; a dedicated powerhead or wavemaker aimed across flat grazing stones is strongly recommended. High dissolved oxygen is non-negotiable — surface agitation must be vigorous. Temperature must be kept in the 20–24°C range; temperatures above 25°C increase disease susceptibility and reduce dissolved O₂ saturation. A biologically mature tank with established algae, diatom, and biofilm colonies on rocks and driftwood is essential before introducing this species; fish routinely starve in newly set-up or sterile tanks. Strong lighting promotes algal growth on hard surfaces.

The species is a constant grazer. Supplement natural biofilm with algae wafers, spirulina tablets, and blanched vegetables placed directly on grazing rocks. Occasional small live or frozen invertebrates (bloodworm, Daphnia) are accepted and beneficial for conditioning.

Keep in groups of four or more; lone specimens or pairs show less natural behaviour, and females are socially dominant. A tight-fitting lid is essential — these loaches are capable climbers and escape readily. Unlike many hillstream loaches, L. disparis does not occupy a permanent personal territory around a single stone; groups range across the tank and should be given ample horizontal surface area of at least 80 cm tank length.

Not suitable for standard warm-water community setups. Best kept in a species-appropriate hillstream biotope or alongside cool-water companions (White Cloud Mountain Minnows, small danios) sharing the same flow and temperature requirements.

[Merge consolidation 2026-06-10]: Consolidated with retired entry fw-fish-396 (Liniparhomaloptera / Liniparhomaloptera disparis disparis). Liniparhomaloptera disparis disparis (nominate subspecies, S China + Vietnam) and L. d. qiongzhongensis (Hainan) are subspecies of the same species; entry covers both at species level.
breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner; reproduction in the wild corresponds to the dry season in southern China and Hong Kong (approximately October–April). In captivity, conditioning with varied high-quality foods and a seasonal temperature drop may trigger spawning. Females are dominant and excavate shallow depressions in substrate or between stones as egg deposition sites. Eggs are approximately 1 mm in diameter, orangish in colour; they hatch in roughly 3 days at 22°C. Fry absorb yolk over a further 3–5 days before emerging at 6–8 mm total length. Sexual maturity is reached within one year. Breeding in the home aquarium is uncommon but has been reported in mature, well-maintained hillstream biotope setups.

primary.name: SeriouslyFish — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
primary.notes: Primary source. Provided temperature (20–24°C), pH (6.5–8.0), hardness (36–268 ppm), native range (Pearl River system, southern China provinces, Vietnam, Hong Kong, Hainan), habitat (fast-flowing oxygenated headwaters, rocky substrate), tank base dimensions (80×30 cm), filter turnover guidance (10–15×), diet (biofilm, algae, small invertebrates), group size (4+), breeding details (seasonal Oct–Apr, egg hatch ~3 days, fry 6–8 mm), and care level (advanced — downgraded to intermediate per project brief).

additional.name: FishBase — Liniparhomaloptera disparis
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (max 8.0 cm TL), temperature range (22–24°C), distribution (Asia: China and Vietnam), habitat (babbling bottom of mountain streams, demersal, freshwater), taxonomy (Gastromyzontidae, Cypriniformes), trophic level 2.7 (herbivore/detritivore). IUCN Least Concern (assessed 2024). No pH, hardness, or depth data available.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Hillstream Loach Care Guide
additional.notes: General hillstream loach guide (primarily Sewellia/reticulated types); confirmed temperature range 20–27°C for hillstream loaches broadly, supplemental feeding with sinking wafers and blanched vegetables, group housing of 3+, tight-fitting lid requirement, and compatibility with peaceful community fish of similar size. No L. disparis-specific data; used for general husbandry corroboration.

fish.breedingNotes: Seasonal spawner (Oct–Apr); females dominant and control egg deposition. Eggs hatch ~3 days; fry emerge at 6–8 mm after yolk absorption. Sexual maturity within one year. Captive breeding uncommon; requires mature hillstream biotope and conditioning.
